title: Add IRuntimeSignalSink and timer signal entry point to AgentBackgroundService
description:
## Source
Council review at `Data/Pages/council/blueprint-yaml-autonomy-council-2026-06-29.md` — Blocking issue B1  
Autonomy design Phase 1

## Background
`AgentBackgroundService.ExecuteAsync` (line ~492-493) launches 3 concurrent tasks: ProcessEventsAsync, DispatchActionsAsync, ChatConsumerAsync. There is no fourth loop for timer signals. The autonomy design requires timer-driven wakeups that follow the same internal path as chat signals, but no `IRuntimeSignalSink` exists and no signal-injection point is wired.

## Scope
1. Define `IRuntimeSignalSink` interface
2. Add a `Channel<RuntimeSignalEnvelope>` to `AgentBackgroundService` 
3. Wire a signal consumer loop (or multiplex into DispatchActionsAsync)
4. Implement timer emitter using `ITimeProvider`
5. Implement suppression cooldowns with named boolean gates
6. Implement wake deduplication (identical wake reasons within cooldown window)
7. Log every wake: reason, goal ID, accepted/suppressed, suppression reason

## Design constraint
All wake paths must funnel through the same runtime entrypoint. Timer signals should be handled as runtime signals, not as a separate planner executor. The signal must be observable in logs, dashboard, and journal entries.

## Deliverables
- `IRuntimeSignalSink` interface
- `Channel<RuntimeSignalEnvelope>` + signal consumer loop
- Timer emitter using `ITimeProvider`
- Suppression cooldowns (named boolean gates, not magic numbers)
- Wake dedup (identical reason within cooldown)
- Structured wake logging
- Unit tests: timer wake → planner, deduplication, cooldown enforcement

## Exit Criteria
- Timer wakes can be simulated in tests using fake `ITimeProvider`
- A timer wake can reach the planner
- No duplicated planning on repeated timer ticks
- Suppression cooldowns prevent wake storms
- All wake activity is visible in logs
